behavior_in_task_description.reason: Tests assert very specific strings not mentioned in instruction.md. For example, test_kill_all_command_exists_in_commands asserts 'const killAll = declareCommand({' and 'Forcefully kill all daemon processes' as exact strings. test_execsync_imported asserts 'import { execSync, spawn } from "child_process";' — the exact import statement format including 'spawn' is not specified in the instructions. test_kill_all_handled_in_program asserts 'if (subcommand === \'kill-all\')' and 'await killAllDaemon

no_solution_leakage.reason: The instruction essentially dictates the exact implementation: specific function names ('killAllDaemons()'), exact command handling patterns ('commandName === \'kill-all\'', 'subcommand === \'kill-all\''), the exact import needed ('execSync from child_process'), the exact algorithm (use 'ps aux' on Unix, PowerShell on Windows, filter 'run-mcp-server' with '--daemon-session', send SIGKILL). This is a detailed specification of the solution, not a description of what's broken.

solution_uniqueness_guard.reason: Tests assert gold-specific implementation details: exact function signature 'async function killAllDaemons(): Promise<void>', exact import format 'import { execSync, spawn } from "child_process";' (requiring spawn in the same import), exact variable naming 'const killAll = declareCommand({', exact conditional format 'if (subcommand === \'kill-all\')'. An alternative correct implementation using a different function name, different import style, or different conditional structure would fail.

tests_verify_behavior_not_text.reason: Almost every test is purely text-matching: reading file contents and checking for specific strings with 'assert X in content'. The only behavioral test is test_typescript_compiles which runs 'npx tsc --noEmit'. No test actually executes the kill-all command, imports the module, or verifies runtime behavior.

test_not_tautological.reason: While the tests are text-based, they do check for specific non-trivial content that wouldn't exist by default in the repo — a stub/empty implementation wouldn't add the required strings to the files. The tests would fail without actual code changes.

anti_cheating_measures.reason: The solution is available at /workspace/playwright (the solve.sh creates a patch). The agent could read /tmp/kill_all.patch or the solution/solve.sh file if accessible. Also, since tests are purely string-matching, the agent could simply insert the exact expected strings into the files without understanding the implementation.

behavior_in_tests.reason: The instruction requires cross-platform process killing behavior (Windows PowerShell, Unix ps aux + SIGKILL), error handling ('Handle errors silently'), and output ('Output the number of processes killed'). None of these runtime behaviors are tested — only text presence in source files.

informative_test_structure.reason: Tests are well-organized with descriptive names (test_kill_all_command_exists_in_commands, test_kill_all_handled_in_program, etc.), docstrings, and section comments separating pass-to-pass from fail-to-pass tests.

structured_data_schema.reason: This task doesn't produce structured data output like JSON/CSV/API/DB.

no_typos.reason: File paths, command names, and variable names in the instruction are consistent and correct.

instruction_no_hint_leakage.reason: The instruction specifies exact file paths, exact function names, exact placement ('In the main program() function - handle commandName === \'kill-all\''), and exact implementation steps. It names the exact buggy lines/locations and what to write there.

config_edit_scope_bounded.reason: The gold patch shows localized diffs to specific files (commands.ts, program.ts, SKILL.md, session-management.md) — not full-file rewrites. Changes are additive and don't contradict existing content.
